name: Furhat
short_description: A tabletop social robot whose human-like face is back-projected onto a translucent mask, built for expressive, multi-party conversation.
long_description: Furhat is a stationary humanoid robot head that projects an animated face from the inside onto a swappable translucent mask, so it can change persona and show expressions, gaze, and lip-sync without mechanical facial actuators. A wide-angle camera, stereo and far-field array microphones, and a three-degree-of-freedom neck let it track and turn toward several people at once, while its SDK and conversation tooling now route dialogue through large language models. Spun out of speech-and-conversation research at KTH in Stockholm, it is a leading research and commercial platform for human-robot interaction, deployed in recruitment, healthcare, education, and customer-facing roles.

images.caption: A Furhat robot head with its animated face back-projected onto a translucent mask.
images.alt_text: A Furhat social robot: a head-and-shoulders bust on a stand with a translucent face mask onto which eyes, eyebrows, nose, and mouth are projected from inside.

interactions.title: Swap its face mid-shift
interactions.description: Lift off the magnetic translucent mask and the projected persona changes with it — the same robot becomes a man, woman, child, or animated character, switching voice and personality without any mechanical parts moving.

interactions.title: It turns to whoever is talking
interactions.description: Sit two or three people in front of it and the head tracks each face, then rotates on its three-axis neck and aims its gaze at whoever currently holds the floor — so a group conversation feels addressed, not broadcast.

interactions.title: Rehearse delivering bad news to a flinching customer
interactions.description: At Rabobank, employees practice difficult conversations by talking to Furhat playing the customer — it holds eye contact, reacts emotionally to how the news is broken, and lets staff fail safely before facing a real person.

interactions.title: Interview the virtual patient who won't cooperate
interactions.description: Medical students take a clinical history from Furhat playing a rheumatology patient — it answers questions through an LLM, simulates the patient's emotions, and adapts to a clumsy or sympathetic bedside manner instead of reading a script.

interactions.title: A robot conducts your first job interview
interactions.description: Built on Furhat as 'Tengai,' the head asks every candidate the same questions in the same order and the same way, then hands anonymized transcripts to human recruiters — an attempt to strip first-impression bias out of screening.

interactions.title: A cooking partner for an older adult
interactions.description: In a Stockholm University project, Furhat coaches seniors through meal prep by voice, learning a person's pace and preferences over repeated sessions so the prompts get less generic and more like a familiar helper.

interactions.title: Notice the quiet person and bring them in
interactions.description: Because it already tracks every face in the group and knows who has and hasn't spoken, it could turn to the person who's gone silent and ask them directly — using its multi-party gaze and far-field mics to balance a lopsided conversation.

interactions.title: Greet returning visitors by mood, not name
interactions.description: As a museum or lobby concierge, it could read a visitor's approach and expression from its camera and open differently for someone who looks lost versus someone in a hurry — grounded in the gaze and vision input it already runs, with no identity database needed.
